clues.clue: Opened in 1912, this Boston ballpark features the 37-foot 'Green Monster' wall.
Opened in 1914 and known for its ivy-covered outfield walls, this Chicago park hosts the Cubs.
College football's 'Iron Bowl' pits Auburn against this Crimson Tide rival.
The Lakers' fiercest NBA-Finals rival plays its home games in this city.
'The Big House,' the largest stadium in the U.S., belongs to this university.
The Baha Men, who asked who let the dogs out, hail from this island nation.
Crazy Frog's 2005 'Axel F' samples the theme from this 1984 Eddie Murphy comedy.
Survivor's 'Eye of the Tiger' was written as the theme for this 1982 boxing sequel.
The 1961 doo-wop hit 'The Lion Sleeps Tonight' adapts a song by this South African, Solomon.
This 1958 Sheb Wooley novelty hit describes a one-eyed, one-horned flying creature that eats people.
This bar of nougat, caramel and peanuts was named for the Mars family's favorite horse.
'Gimme a break' advertises this originally-British four-finger wafer bar.
Hershey's introduced these foil-wrapped conical chocolates in 1907.
This Mars bar's name references swashbuckling characters and once contained three flavored pieces.
Toblerone's triangular peaks evoke the Matterhorn in this country, where the bar is made.
The cosmic villain Thanos was created in 1973 at this publisher.
Jack Kirby created the New Gods and Darkseid at this publisher.
Alan Moore's 1986 limited series 'Watchmen' was published by this company.
The mature-readers imprint 'Vertigo,' home to 'Sandman,' belonged to this publisher.
Todd McFarlane's 1992 hit 'Spawn' helped launch this creator-owned publisher.
A single Cheerio takes the shape of this letter of the alphabet.
Each piece of the Post cereal 'Honeycomb' takes this six-sided geometric form.
Cocoa Pebbles and Fruity Pebbles are licensed from this prehistoric animated family.
This strawberry-flavored General Mills monster cereal debuted in 1971 alongside Count Chocula.
Lucky Charms launched in 1964 with marshmallow hearts, moons, stars and these green ones.

final.clue: This Apple co-founder engineered the Apple I and Apple II and is the company's other 'Steve.'
